title: Rotate logs/turns.jsonl: unbounded append-only log needs nightly size-based rotation
current_state: logs/turns.jsonl is append-only with no rotation - it grows unbounded. Every turn in every mode logs there by design ('log everything' is a standing rule - rotation must not gate or drop logging). A rotation pattern already exists in the repo: scripts/passive/replication_log_rotate.py. The daemon scheduler (tools/tools_scheduler.py, APScheduler) already runs nightly jobs (memory prune, briefing).
target_state: A nightly scheduler job rotates logs/turns.jsonl when it exceeds a size threshold (~50MB): rename to logs/turns-YYYYMMDD.jsonl and let the logger start a fresh file. Old segments are never deleted (no-delete rule; disk is cheap, reversibility is mandatory). ~20 lines modeled on replication_log_rotate.py.
migration_plan: 1. Add a rotate function modeled on scripts/passive/replication_log_rotate.py: if os.path.getsize(logs/turns.jsonl) > threshold, os.replace to logs/turns-YYYYMMDD.jsonl (append -1, -2 suffix if same-day rotation recurs).
2. Register it in the existing nightly APScheduler block in tools/tools_scheduler.py alongside the memory prune job.
3. Confirm agent/turn_log.py reopens/creates the file per append (if it holds a persistent handle, rotation must happen through a path the logger tolerates - check before choosing rename semantics).
4. Test: testing/test_turns_rotation.py - write a temp jsonl over a tiny threshold, run the rotate function, assert renamed segment exists and a fresh append still works (about 12 lines).
risk_notes: The one real trap is a persistent file handle in agent/turn_log.py on Windows (rename of an open file fails on win32) - verify how the logger opens the file first; if it holds a handle, rotate at daemon startup instead of mid-run. Threshold is a constant, not a config knob.
